title: Kakavia
subtitle: Greek fisherman's soup with potatoes and olive oil
excerpt: A simple, robust fish soup made from whole fish or robust fillets simmered with potatoes, onion, celery, tomatoes, and lots of olive oil. The broth is enriched by the fish themselves and finished with lemon. Often called the Greek bouillabaisse, though its logic is much simpler.
sourceNotes: Kakavia (κακαβιά) is documented from ancient Greek sources as a fishermen's pot, whatever fish was caught simmered in sea water with olive oil. The modern version uses fresh water, stock, and vegetables. It is considered the precursor to French bouillabaisse, both tracing to Greek maritime cooking along the Mediterranean coast. The dish is associated with the Greek islands and coastal villages.

content.text: Kakavia is one of the oldest recorded dishes in the world, named for the kakavi (κακάβι), the three-legged bronze pot used by Greek fishermen to cook whatever they caught at sea. The modern version is more refined but follows the same logic: fish, potatoes, oil, and whatever aromatics are on hand, simmered briefly in a light stock or water. The fish finishes the cooking in 8 to 10 minutes; do not add it early or it will overcook.

Use the most affordable whole fish or firm fillets available: sea bream, sea bass, mackerel, or any small rockfish. For a home version, large fillets work as well as whole fish; the bones and head of a whole fish do improve the broth, but the dish is pleasant either way.

items.name: Sea bream
items.prepNote: scaled and gutted, or use 600 g firm white fish fillets

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into thick rounds

content.text: Pour the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} into a large pot. Add the {{onion}}, {{celery}}, {{carrot}}, {{garlic}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Pour in 1 litre of water and season well with salt. Bring to a boil, then add the {{potato}} and {{tomato}}. Simmer for 20 minutes until the potatoes are nearly tender.

Lay the {{sea-bream}} fish or fillets on top of the vegetables. Cover and simmer gently for 8 to 10 minutes until the fish is just cooked through and flaking. Add the {{lemon}} juice, scatter {{parsley-flat}} over, and taste for seasoning. Serve in deep bowls with crusty bread.

items.fix: Add fish only in the last 8 to 10 minutes; simmer gently, a rolling boil breaks up delicate fillets
items.cause: Added too early or cooked at a boil
items.symptom: Fish has overcooked and fallen apart

items.fix: Use 1 litre for 4 servings; the fish and olive oil enrich the broth sufficiently without additional stock
items.cause: Too much water for the quantity of fish

items.fix: 80 ml is the correct amount; add it all at the beginning so it emulsifies into the broth during cooking
20 minutes is enough for the potato round; if they start breaking down, add the fish immediately and serve
items.cause: Over-simmered before the fish was added

content.text: Kakavia is the dish that ties modern Greek cuisine to its ancient roots. Same pot, same logic of whatever was caught that day, cooked quickly in water with oil and aromatics. Greek colonists brought their cooking habits along the Mediterranean coast. Versions of the simple fish pot became the precursor to French bouillabaisse, Italian cacciucco, and Spanish sopa de pescado. The Greek original stays simpler than its descendants. No rouille, no saffron, no toasted croutons. Just fish, vegetables, olive oil, and lemon.
